summary: DeVault documents his frustrating experience purchasing a RaptorCS POWER9 Blackbird system, detailing months of hardware failures and unresponsive support. The $2,500+ investment arrived without ordered RAM, boot-looped due to apparent hardware defects, and support tickets went unanswered for weeks at a time. After publishing this scathing review, RaptorCS reached out, apologized, identified a faulty CPU, shipped a replacement, and implemented new support mechanisms including a self-service RMA system. The post was later retitled and followed up with a positive review once the machine was working.
key_points: The Blackbird order took 8 months to ship and arrived missing the purchased RAM
The system exhibited persistent boot failures that months of support tickets and self-diagnosis couldn't resolve, ultimately traced to a faulty CPU
RaptorCS support was essentially non-responsive, with weeks-long gaps between replies and no resolution over nearly 3 months
Public pressure from the blog post prompted rapid response: RaptorCS apologized, shipped a replacement CPU, refunded the missing RAM, and implemented systemic support improvements
Other Blackbird owners reported similar reliability and support frustrations in IRC
money_quotes: So, 10 months after I placed an order for a POWER9 machine, 3 months after I received it (without the RAM I purchased, no less), and over $2,500 invested… it's clear that buying the Blackbird was an expensive mistake.
If I do, I doubt the "support" team will have been involved.
Currently my best bet seems to be waiting for some apparent staff member (the only apparent staff member) who idles in the IRC channel on Freenode and allegedly comes online from time to time.
It's too large and unwieldly to be an effective paper weight, either!
my personal theory is that they have really bad ticket software that 'loses' tickets somehow
I agree that this shows positive improvements and a willingness to continue making improvements in their support experience.
key_insight: Even hardware vendors aligned with free software ideals can fail their customers through poor support processes, but public accountability and willingness to improve can redeem the relationship.
